turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Today's ruling, make no mistake, threatens the very essence of public education in this country. Exposure to new and different ideas has always been a vital part of public education, until now. Casting aside centuries of precedent, the Court invents a new constitutional right of parents to avoid exposure by their children to new ideas and concepts that may conflict with a parent's religious belief. And yes, what they're objecting to is that exposing students to the very fact that gay and transgender people exist and that their loved ones may celebrate their life events, the majority proclaims, is enough to be prohibited by the Constitution. That novel rule contorts this Court's precedent and invites utter chaos for public schools. Given the great diversity of religious beliefs in this country, countless interactions that occur every day in public school might expose children to messages that conflict with a parent's religious beliefs. Books about the theory of evolution, women's rights, interfaith marriage, divorce, and endless other topics are all potential free exercise violation under this Court's new test. Excising and limiting instruction to avoid all those topics will create an administrative nightmare. To understand where the Court goes wrong, start with the record, which they don't. By the majority's telling, this case is about a deliberate campaign to pressure children to give up their religious beliefs. Do not be misled. The actual record tells a different story. This case centers on five picture books featuring gay and transgender characters. The Montgomery County School Board added those books into its English language curriculum after realizing that many students and families in the community, including those with gay and transgender family members, were not represented in school reading materials. The five books at issue present the same kind of narrative and themes as many other classic children's books, stories about overcoming differences, fairy tale romances, and celebrating big life events like weddings. Take, for example, Uncle Bobby's Wedding. This picture book tells the story of a young girl, Chloe, and her favorite Uncle Bobby. When Uncle Bobby gets engaged to his boyfriend, the whole family is overjoyed, except for Chloe. She worries that she won't be able to keep having fun together with Uncle Bobby once he gets married. But Uncle Bobby assures her that they'll still spend some time together, and that he and his fiance take Chloe out on adventures to prove it. On the day of the wedding, Chloe is incredibly happy, and the night ends with everyone dancing together. The other four books at issue play on similar themes. To the extent there is a common thread across all of them, it is a simple one. Gay and transgender people exist, and their family members and close friends may treat them with love and kindness. That includes Born Again.  The Board instructed schools to use these books like any other in English class, and there was no planned instruction on gender and sexuality. The Board also expressly prohibited teachers from asking any child to change their beliefs and encouraged teachers to foster civility and respect including towards religious students, in response to questions about the books. The Board's goal, in short, was to create a more inclusive and supportive environment for students of all backgrounds. The majority tells a contrary tale by plucking quotations out of context and omitting critical parts of the record. The teacher guidance the majority discusses, for instance, does not accuse students of being hurtful for asking questions based on their religious beliefs. The quoted language offers guidance to teachers on how to respond if a student calls gay or transgender kids weird or makes comments like, "That's so gay." The Board rightly encourages teachers to respond to comments like that by letting the student know that their words may be hurtful to others as the majority should agree. Similarly, the majority seizes on statements individual Board members made outside of any official proceeding and takes their words out of context. The majority also glosses over what parents are actually objecting to here. This is what the parents say in their complaint. It's not what the majority claims. They claim, the parents, that, "Exposing their children to activities and curriculum that undermine their religious beliefs will prohibit the free exercise of their religion." Accordingly, the question before this Court is simple, does mere exposure to concepts and ideas that conflict with a parent's religious beliefs prohibit the free exercise of their religion? The answer to that question has been for centuries, no. For the entirety of this nation's history, the Constitution has never guaranteed citizens a right to avoid ideas with which they disagree, even on religious grounds. There is no public school exception to those principles. The Court made that clear in West Virginia versus Barnett over 80 years ago, and as recently as two years ago in Kennedy versus Bremerton School District. The bottom-line of our cases is that mere exposure to objectionable ideas does not amount to a prohibition on the free exercise of religion. That well-established principle should have made this an easy case. The parents, after all, objected to mere exposure, meaning their claim runs headlong into First Amendment doctrine. So how does the majority get around that clear answer? Rather than follow this Court's unambiguous precedent, the majority rescues petitioners' exposure to theory by renaming it. The parents' free exercise rights are burdened, the majority claims, because reading the storybooks out loud and in class creates, "a very real threat of undermining" the religious belief that the parents wish to instill in their children. That is simply exposure by another name. So from where does the majority derive its novel threat test? This Court's ruling in Yoder, the majority says, established it over a half century ago, unbeknownst to any Court of appeals in the nation, and until today, unbeknownst to this Court as well. The majority's reading of Yoder is dead wrong. In that case, the Court held that Wisconsin could not force Amish parents to send their children to traditional high school all day when their faith required that they keep their children at home in order to integrate them into the Amish community. The Court thus did not establish any form of threat test or suggest that mere exposure can constitute a free exercise burden. To the contrary, the Court's ruling was based on coercion to act contrary to one's faith, as this Court's later cases have expressly confirmed. The majority's analogy to Yoder is also implausible on its face. The Court in Yoder detailed at length the record Amish evidence that compulsory high school attendance would result in the destruction of the Amish church, because the parents could not keep their children at home to teach them their faith and integrate them into the Amish community at a critical time in their children's young lives. Today, the majority declares that reading out loud storybooks like Uncle Bobby's Wedding or Born Again is just like the law in Yoder. That assertion is remarkable. Reading a picture book that portrays a family's happiness at the news of their gay son's engagement, or reading about a parent who accepts a declaration by a child and tries to make life easier for them, that, the majority asserts, is equivalent to a law that threatened the very survival of the Amish community in the United States. The absurdity of that claim and the intolerance to gay and transgender people that it reveals is chilling. It bears emphasis, too, that the parents here do not object to sending their children to public school, and they remain free to teach their religious beliefs and practices to their children at home. That makes this case decidedly unlike Yoder, where the parents were barred from engaging in their required religious practice with their children at home. The Court's own application of its tests, moreover, proves that it lacks any discernible limits. If innocuous children's book with implicit normative messages are enough to trigger strict scrutiny under our law, then nearly everything is. Countless topics and books will be subject to free exercise challenges. The Court offers no reason why its tests would be limited to reading material either. Interactions with teachers and students could presumably involve implicit messages that parents may find contrary to their religious beliefs. A female teacher displaying a wedding photo with her wife, a student's presentation on her family tree, featuring her gay parents, or a child's art display that says, "Love is love." These examples do not involve one class, one subject. They are interwoven into curricula, and to say that this will not cause an administrative burden is just ridiculous. The majority identifies no principal basis on which to distinguish these cases from this one. One thing is clear, however, the damage to America's public school system will be profound. Public schools already struggling with teacher shortages and tight budgets will have to become experts in religious doctrine to provide notice to parents of any material they might object to on religious grounds, and then track exactly which lessons and books every individual parent wants their kids insulated from. Teachers will have to stop classes midway to usher students in and out, depending on what books or discussion topics come up. These administrative burdens could cripple schools, and destroy the openness of a public school education. For the many school districts that cannot afford to take on these burdens, or who fear being sued by angry parents if they mess up, the only option might be to strip from their curriculum any material that parents might object to on religious grounds. The Court ruling, in effect, hands a subset of parents the right to veto curricular choices long left to locally elected school Boards. Storybooks with gay and transgender characters are declared out of bounds today. Books on evolution, interfaith marriage, and history may be next. There have already been lower court decisions where parents in the past have raised these issues. The majority's decision, I fear, will be the undoing of our public school system and the very engine of our democracy. Unable to condone that grave misjudgment, I, with Justices Kagan and Jackson dissent.
